revision.comment: Bot: mark 1 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24167314344|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:中和院]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|1=Heian-kyō}}}}
[[File:平安宮 中和院位置.png|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} {{ill|Heian Palace|en|Heian Palace|qid=Q304501|lt=Heian Palace|1=Heian Palace}}, showing the location of Chūkain (中和院)]] '''Chūkain''' (中和院/中院), also known as '''Nakain''' (中院), was a ceremonial hall located within the {{ill|Heian Palace|ja|平安宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Palace|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大内裏|1=Heian Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q304501}} (Daidairi) in {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}. The site corresponds approximately to the current vicinity of Senbon Street and Shimodachiuri Street in {{ill|Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321824|lt=Kamigyō-ku|1=Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto}}, Kyoto. == Overview == Chūkain was situated in the southwestern section of the {{ill|Inner Palace|ja|内裏外郭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135550949}} within the Heian Palace. It was the site for the Emperor's personal participation in rituals such as the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|qid=Q11501518|lt=Niinamesai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} (Harvest Festival, held annually in November) and the {{ill|Kamimiso|ja|神今食|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimiso|1=Jinkonjiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55535655}} (divine meals held in June and December). Constructed during the founding of the palace, it is referred to in historical records as "Chūkain," "Nakain," "Kamimiso-in," and "Saien." The compound was enclosed by earthen walls and contained the '''Shinkaden''' (神嘉殿) as its main hall, along with the East and West Halls (東舎・西舎) and the North Hall (北殿). Corridors connected the Shinkaden to the other structures. The central area of the Shinkaden was partitioned to create a sacred space for rituals, where the Emperor symbolically shared food with {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}. A similar ritual is still conducted at the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|qid=Q500681|lt=Kōkyū|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}} in Tokyo within the modern {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}}, which includes a Shinkaden. Chūkain was the first permanent ritual space to be separated from the emperor’s residential quarters, a significant development from earlier practices at {{ill|Nagaoka-kyō|en|Nagaoka-kyō|qid=Q1052803|lt=Nagaoka-kyō|1=Nagaoka-kyō}}. Its central location within the Heian Palace reflects Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|qid=Q314846|lt=Kanmu|1=Emperor Kanmu}}'s vision of a ceremonial layout linking ancestral deities, the Emperor’s residence, and the symbolic presence of a retired emperor.<ref name="Hashimoto">Hashimoto Yoshinori, "The Structure of the Heian Palace," *Visiting the Heian Palace Ruins*, Kyoto Sōbunsya, 2011.</ref> == History == The first documented reference to Nakain appears in {{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|qid=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki|1=Nihon Kōki}} in 804, noting the collapse of its western tower. This suggests it existed during the initial construction of the Heian Palace. Some scholars propose that the "Chū" in the name signifies its central location, while others link it to the "Nakatomi" clan, known for its Shinto rituals. Records such as {{ill|Ruijū Kokushi|en|Ruijū Kokushi|qid=Q1038639|lt=Ruiju Kokushi|1=Ruijū Kokushi}} from 831 also use the name "Chūkain," but some scholars argue that the name became standardized after the Great Fire of 960 and the subsequent era of {{ill|Ōwa|en|Ōwa|qid=Q1324768|lt=Ōwa|1=Ōwa}}. alternate_name such as "Kamimiso-in" (神今食院) and "Saien" (斎院) appear in documents like the {{ill|Jōgan Gishiki|ja|貞観儀式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan Gishiki|1=Jōgan Gishiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11634544}} and {{ill|Saigū Nikki|ja|西宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigū Nikki|1=Seikyūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11627667}}. In 1058, a major fire destroyed the 11 Imperial Palace and Chūkain, marking the first destruction of the Shinkaden since the capital's relocation to Heian-kyō. Subsequent fires in 1112 and 1163 also affected the site.<ref name="Hashimoto" /> Archaeological excavations conducted in the late 20th century revealed distinct stratigraphy and scattered tiles, confirming the location of ceremonial structures. The uncovered dimensions of the Shinkaden and associated findings such as volcanic tuff foundations highlight the site's architectural significance as a ritual center.<ref name="Hashimoto" /> == Structure == [[File:平安宮中和院跡出土 軒丸瓦.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Green-glazed roof tile from Chūkain|qq=}}{{small|Exhibited at the {{ill|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|en|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|qid=Q11375124|lt=Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|1=Kyoto City Archaeological Museum}}.|qq=}}]] Chūkain's central structure, the '''Shinkaden''', was surrounded by four enclosed walls and connected to auxiliary buildings via corridors. The Shinkaden was an east-west-oriented building with a layout of seven bays in width and two in depth, surrounded by covered galleries. The interior was divided into three sections, with the central area serving as the sacred space (神殿), the western section for the Emperor's seat, and the eastern section for attendants. The East and West Halls, oriented north-south, were four bays in width and two in depth, with adjacent galleries. The North Hall, oriented east-west, was seven bays in width and two in depth, also featuring galleries.<ref name="Hashimoto" /> Excavations of the Shinkaden reveal it measured approximately 40 meters east-to-west and was built on a foundation of dressed volcanic tuff. The structure’s lack of extensive roofing tiles indicates its primary use as a ceremonial site. According to legend, the current {{ill|Isonokami Shrine|en|Isonokami Shrine|qid=Q708859|lt=Isonokami Shrine|1=Isonokami Jingū}} worship hall was relocated from the Shinkaden in 1081, although architectural evidence dates it to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}.<ref>Miyazawa Satoshi, "Isonokami Shrine," *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha.</ref> == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Heian Palace|ja|平安宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Palace|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大内裏|1=Heian Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q304501}} * {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|qid=Q314846|lt=Kanmu Emperor|1=Emperor Kanmu}} * {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|qid=Q11501518|lt=Niinamesai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} {{translated page|ja|中和院|version=92450690|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:中和院]]
<!--Chukain Temple--><!--Chukain Temple--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q109359281}} [[Category:上京区の歴史]] [[Category:平安宮]] [[Category:Pages with deleted QID in ill template]]
